mitre_attack: T1003.001 - OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory
T1555.003 - Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers
T1552.001 - Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
cwe: CWE-316 - Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in Memory
CWE-312 - Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information

threat.title: Memory Dump Credential and Secret Extraction
threat.description: When applications store credentials, secrets, or sensitive data in plaintext within process memory without proper protection, attackers with local or remote access can extract this information through memory dumps, process injection, or exploiting memory disclosure vulnerabilities. This includes LSASS credential harvesting, browser password extraction, database connection string leakage, and API key exposure from heap dumps. Common attack vectors include Mimikatz for Windows credential theft, process memory scraping for browser passwords, and crash dump analysis revealing plaintext secrets.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains initial access via phishing or vulnerability exploitation (e.g., remote code execution)
2. Escalates privileges to SYSTEM/root level or leverages SeDebugPrivilege
3. Uses Mimikatz, ProcDump, or custom tools to dump LSASS process memory (lsass.exe on Windows)
4. Extracts plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets from memory dump
5. Discovers database connection strings with credentials in application process dumps
6. Uses stolen credentials for lateral movement, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration across environment
prerequisites: Local or remote access to target system with administrative privileges
Ability to execute memory dumping tools (Mimikatz, ProcDump, GDB)
Target application stores credentials in plaintext memory
No memory encryption or credential protection mechanisms enabled
LSASS or browser processes running with sensitive credentials cached

cve_references.description: Heartbleed - OpenSSL memory disclosure allowing extraction of private keys, passwords, and session tokens from server memory
Meltdown - Speculative execution vulnerability allowing unauthorized memory reads including credentials and encryption keys
glibc realpath() buffer underflow leading to memory disclosure and potential credential exposure
PostgreSQL 'COPY TO/FROM PROGRAM' allowing arbitrary code execution and memory dump access
HiveNightmare/SeriousSAM - Windows SAM database access allowing credential hash extraction from memory dumps

real_world_breaches.incident: Mimikatz LSASS Credential Harvesting in Target Breach
real_world_breaches.impact: 40 million credit cards and 70 million customer records compromised; $18.5 million settlement
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers used Mimikatz to extract plaintext credentials from LSASS memory after gaining initial access via HVAC vendor credentials

real_world_breaches.incident: NotPetya Credential Dumping for Lateral Movement
real_world_breaches.impact: $10 billion in global damages; Maersk lost $300 million alone
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Malware used credential dumping tools including Mimikatz to extract LSASS credentials and spread rapidly across corporate networks

real_world_breaches.incident: SolarWinds Supply Chain Attack - Memory Credential Theft
real_world_breaches.impact: 18,000+ organizations compromised including US government agencies
real_world_breaches.root_cause: SUNBURST backdoor dumped LSASS memory to steal credentials and move laterally through victim networks

real_world_breaches.impact: Researchers demonstrated extraction of master passwords from Chrome process memory enabling bulk password theft
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Chrome stores decrypted passwords in process memory without proper protection, allowing memory scraping

real_world_breaches.incident: MongoDB Connection String Exposure in Heap Dumps
real_world_breaches.impact: Database credentials leaked via Java heap dumps containing connection strings with embedded passwords
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Applications logged full MongoDB URIs (mongodb://user:password@host) that persisted in memory and crash dumps

bug_bounty_reports.summary: LSASS memory dump via process injection allowing domain admin credential extraction
Browser password extraction from process memory via custom memory scanning tool

security_research.title: Mimikatz: A Little Tool to Play with Windows Security
security_research.key_finding: Windows stores plaintext credentials in LSASS memory by default, enabling trivial extraction with SeDebugPrivilege

security_research.title: Credential Dumping Techniques and Mitigations
security_research.key_finding: LSASS memory dumping is the most common credential theft technique used in 90% of ransomware attacks

security_research.title: Memory Protection Best Practices for Sensitive Data
security_research.key_finding: Applications must zero memory containing cryptographic keys and credentials immediately after use

mitigations.title: Enable Windows Credential Guard (LSASS Protection)
mitigations.description: Windows Credential Guard uses virtualization-based security (VBS) to isolate LSASS secrets in a protected environment that's inaccessible to the operating system. This prevents credential dumping tools like Mimikatz from extracting plaintext passwords, NTLM hashes, and Kerberos tickets.

code_example.code:
# Enable Credential Guard via Group Policy
# Path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Device Guard
# Setting: Turn On Virtualization Based Security
# Set: Enabled
# Credential Guard Configuration: Enabled with UEFI lock

# OR via Registry (requires reboot)
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' `
  -Name 'LsaCfgFlags' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# Enable VBS and Credential Guard via PowerShell
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceGuard' `
  -Name 'EnableVirtualizationBasedSecurity' -Value 1

Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\DeviceGuard' `
  -Name 'RequirePlatformSecurityFeatures' -Value 3 # 1=TPM, 3=TPM+SecureBoot

Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' `
  -Name 'LsaCfgFlags' -Value 1 # 1=Enabled with UEFI lock, 2=Enabled without lock

# Verify Credential Guard is running
Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard

# Check for VBS and Credential Guard status
$DeviceGuard = Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\Microsoft\Windows\DeviceGuard
if ($DeviceGuard.SecurityServicesRunning -contains 1) {
    Write-Host "Credential Guard is RUNNING" -ForegroundColor Green
} else {
    Write-Host "Credential Guard is NOT RUNNING" -ForegroundColor Red
}

# Enable LSA Protection (PPL - Protected Process Light)
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' `
  -Name 'RunAsPPL' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# Disable WDigest (prevents plaintext password caching)
Set-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest' `
  -Name 'UseLogonCredential' -Value 0

Write-Host "Reboot required to apply changes" -ForegroundColor Yellow

iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled
validation_steps: Verify hardware meets requirements (UEFI 2.3.1+, TPM 2.0, Virtualization Extensions)
Enable VBS and Credential Guard in Group Policy or Registry
Reboot system and verify Credential Guard is running
Test with Mimikatz - should fail to extract plaintext passwords
Monitor Event ID 4611 (Trusted Boot) and 4625 (Failed Logon)

mitigations.title: Implement Memory Encryption with DPAPI and SecureString
mitigations.description: Use Windows Data Protection API (DPAPI) to encrypt sensitive data in memory and .NET SecureString for credential handling. SecureString stores encrypted strings and zeros memory immediately after use, preventing credential exposure in memory dumps.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
validation_steps: Replace String with SecureString for password variables
Implement DPAPI encryption for sensitive configuration data
Add memory zeroing (Array.Clear) after sensitive data use
Test memory dump extraction - credentials should be encrypted
Use memory profiler to verify plaintext credentials don't persist

mitigations.title: Use Linux Kernel Keyring for Credential Storage
mitigations.description: Linux kernel keyring provides secure in-kernel storage for credentials that's protected from userspace memory dumps. Keys are stored in kernel memory with strict access controls and automatic expiration.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Install keyutils library (apt install keyutils)
Store credentials in kernel keyring instead of process memory
Set appropriate key timeouts and permissions
Test memory dump - credentials should not be visible
Verify key revocation works correctly

mitigations.title: Enable LSASS Process Protection and Audit Logging
mitigations.description: Configure LSASS as a Protected Process Light (PPL) and enable comprehensive audit logging to detect credential dumping attempts. PPL prevents non-PPL processes from accessing LSASS memory even with administrative privileges.

code_example.code:
# Enable LSASS as Protected Process Light (PPL)
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa' `
  -Name 'RunAsPPL' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# Enable LSASS audit logging
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\LSASS.exe' `
  -Name 'AuditLevel' -Value 8 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# Configure advanced audit policy for credential access
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Credential Validation" /success:enable /failure:enable
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Sensitive Privilege Use" /success:enable /failure:enable
auditpol /set /subcategory:"Process Creation" /success:enable

# Enable command-line auditing (detect Mimikatz usage)
New-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Audit' `
  -Name 'ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled' -Value 1 -PropertyType DWORD -Force

# Configure Sysmon for detailed process monitoring
# Download Sysmon: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/sysmon

# Sysmon configuration for LSASS monitoring
$sysmonConfig = @'
<Sysmon schemaversion="4.82">
  <EventFiltering>
    <!-- Detect LSASS memory access -->
    <ProcessAccess onmatch="include">
      <TargetImage condition="is">C:\Windows\System32\lsass.exe</TargetImage>
    </ProcessAccess>
    
    <!-- Detect credential dumping tools -->
    <ProcessCreate onmatch="include">
      <CommandLine condition="contains any">mimikatz;procdump;lsass;sekurlsa</CommandLine>
      <CommandLine condition="contains">comsvcs.dll,MiniDump</CommandLine>
    </ProcessCreate>
    
    <!-- Detect suspicious DLL loads -->
    <ImageLoad onmatch="include">
      <ImageLoaded condition="contains any">vaultcli.dll;samlib.dll</ImageLoaded>
    </ImageLoad>
  </EventFiltering>
</Sysmon>
'@

$sysmonConfig | Out-File -FilePath "C:\sysmon-config.xml" -Encoding UTF8

# Install Sysmon with configuration
sysmon64.exe -accepteula -i C:\sysmon-config.xml

# Create scheduled task to monitor LSASS dumps
$action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "powershell.exe" -Argument @'
-Command "Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Security';ID=4656,4663} | Where-Object {$_.Message -like '*lsass.exe*'} | ForEach-Object {Send-MailMessage -To 'security@company.com' -From 'alerts@company.com' -Subject 'LSASS Access Detected' -Body $_.Message -SmtpServer 'smtp.company.com'}"
'@

$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtStartup
Register-ScheduledTask -TaskName "LSASS-Monitor" -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest

Write-Host "LSASS protection and monitoring enabled. Reboot required." -ForegroundColor Green

# Verify PPL is enabled after reboot
function Test-LsassProtection {
    $lsassProcess = Get-Process lsass
    $protectionLevel = (Get-Process -Id $lsassProcess.Id).ProtectionLevel
    
    if ($protectionLevel -ne "None") {
        Write-Host "LSASS is running as Protected Process: $protectionLevel" -ForegroundColor Green
        return $true
    } else {
        Write-Host "WARNING: LSASS is NOT protected!" -ForegroundColor Red
        return $false
    }
}

# Test-LsassProtection

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect anomalies
PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled
validation_steps: Enable LSASS PPL via registry (RunAsPPL=1)
Install and configure Sysmon for LSASS monitoring
Enable advanced audit policies for credential access
Test with Mimikatz - should fail with ACCESS_DENIED
Verify Event IDs 4656, 4663, 10 (Sysmon) are logged for LSASS access

mitigations.title: Implement Secrets Management with Azure Key Vault and Managed Identity
mitigations.description: Store all application secrets, connection strings, and API keys in Azure Key Vault with Managed Identity authentication. This eliminates the need to store credentials in application memory or configuration files.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Create Azure Key Vault and enable RBAC
Assign Managed Identity to application (VM/App Service)
Grant 'Key Vault Secrets User' role to Managed Identity
Remove all secrets from appsettings.json and environment variables
Test application retrieves secrets successfully from Key Vault
Verify process memory dump contains no plaintext secrets

mitigations.title: Deploy EDR with Memory Scanning and Behavioral Detection
mitigations.description: Deploy Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions that monitor for credential dumping techniques, LSASS memory access, and suspicious process behavior. EDR can detect and block Mimikatz and similar tools in real-time.

code_example.code:
# Microsoft Defender for Endpoint - Custom Detection Rules
# Rule 1: Detect LSASS memory dumping
apiVersion: v1
kind: CustomDetection
metadata:
  name: lsass-memory-dump-detection
  severity: High
spec:
  query: |
    DeviceProcessEvents
    | where FileName in ("procdump.exe", "procdump64.exe", "mimikatz.exe", "taskmgr.exe")
      or ProcessCommandLine has_any ("lsass", "lsass.exe", "lsass.dmp")
      or ProcessCommandLine contains "comsvcs.dll,MiniDump"
    | where ProcessCommandLine contains "lsass"
    | project
        Timestamp,
        DeviceName,
        InitiatingProcessAccountName,
        FileName,
        ProcessCommandLine,
        InitiatingProcessFileName,
        InitiatingProcessCommandLine
  actions:
    - alert:
        title: "LSASS Memory Dump Attempt Detected"
        description: "Suspicious process attempting to access LSASS memory"
    - isolate_device: true
    - kill_process: true

# Rule 2: Detect Mimikatz execution
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: CustomDetection
metadata:
  name: mimikatz-execution-detection
  severity: Critical
spec:
  query: |
    DeviceProcessEvents
    | where ProcessCommandLine has_any (
        "sekurlsa::logonpasswords",
        "sekurlsa::tickets",
        "lsadump::sam",
        "privilege::debug",
        "kerberos::golden",
        "vault::cred"
      )
      or FileName =~ "mimikatz.exe"
      or SHA256 in (
        "8a7c0e0d3c9e1f2b3a4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7e8f9a0b1c2d3e4f5a6b",
        "known_mimikatz_hashes"
      )
    | join kind=leftouter (
        DeviceNetworkEvents
        | where RemoteUrl contains "github.com/gentilkiwi"
      ) on DeviceId
    | project
        Timestamp,
        DeviceName,
        AccountName,
        FileName,
        ProcessCommandLine,
        SHA256
  actions:
    - alert:
        title: "Mimikatz Credential Dumping Tool Detected"
        severity: Critical
    - isolate_device: true
    - collect_forensics: true

# Rule 3: Detect browser credential theft
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: CustomDetection
metadata:
  name: browser-credential-access
  severity: Medium
spec:
  query: |
    DeviceFileEvents
    | where FolderPath has_any (
        "\\Google\\Chrome\\User Data\\Default\\Login Data",
        "\\Mozilla\\Firefox\\Profiles\\.default\\logins.json",
        "\\Microsoft\\Edge\\User Data\\Default\\Login Data"
      )
      and ActionType in ("FileCreated", "FileModified", "FileRead")
      and InitiatingProcessFileName !in ("chrome.exe", "firefox.exe", "msedge.exe")
    | project
        Timestamp,
        DeviceName,
        InitiatingProcessAccountName,
        InitiatingProcessFileName,
        FileName,
        FolderPath,
        ActionType
  actions:
    - alert:
        title: "Suspicious Browser Credential Store Access"
        description: "Non-browser process accessing credential database"

# Defender ATP Advanced Hunting Query (KQL)
# Save as scheduled query to run every 15 minutes
---
# Query: Detect LSASS access with SeDebugPrivilege
DeviceEvents
| where ActionType == "ProcessAccess"
| where TargetProcessFileName =~ "lsass.exe"
| extend AccessRights = tostring(AdditionalFields.AccessRights)
| where AccessRights has_any ("0x1010", "0x1410", "0x1438")
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceProcessEvents
    | where ProcessTokenElevation == "TokenElevationTypeFull"
  ) on DeviceId, InitiatingProcessId
| summarize
    AccessCount = count(),
    FirstAccess = min(Timestamp),
    LastAccess = max(Timestamp),
    UniqueProcesses = dcount(InitiatingProcessFileName)
  by DeviceName, InitiatingProcessAccountName, InitiatingProcessFileName
| where AccessCount > 3
| order by AccessCount desc

iso27001_controls: A.16.1.4 - Assessment and decision on information security events
nist_csf: DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized personnel activity
RS.AN-1 - Notifications from detection systems are investigated
validation_steps: Deploy EDR agent to all endpoints
Configure custom detection rules for LSASS access
Enable automatic response actions (isolate, kill process)
Test detection with Mimikatz simulation (in lab environment)
Verify alerts trigger and response actions execute
Configure SIEM integration for centralized monitoring

indicators: Unexpected LSASS memory access by non-system processes
Process dumps created for lsass.exe or browser processes
Execution of known credential dumping tools (Mimikatz, ProcDump, Invoke-Mimikatz)
Large memory reads from lsass.exe process space
Creation of .dmp files in unusual directories
Suspicious PowerShell commands accessing LSASS memory
Network connections from credential manager processes
Browser credential database access by non-browser processes
Crash dumps containing sensitive connection strings or API keys

log_queries.query:
// Detect LSASS memory dump attempts
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4656 // Handle to object was requested
| where ObjectName contains "lsass.exe"
| where AccessMask in ("0x1010", "0x1410", "0x1438") // PROCESS_VM_READ, PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION
| extend ProcessName = tostring(split(ProcessName, '\\')[-1])
| where ProcessName !in ("svchost.exe", "csrss.exe", "wininit.exe")
| summarize
    AccessAttempts = count(),
    FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated),
    LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated)
  by Computer, Account, ProcessName, ObjectName
| where AccessAttempts > 3
| order by AccessAttempts desc

// Detect Mimikatz execution via command line
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688 // Process creation
| where CommandLine has_any (
    "sekurlsa::logonpasswords",
    "sekurlsa::tickets",
    "lsadump::sam",
    "privilege::debug",
    "vault::cred",
    "kerberos::golden"
  )
  or NewProcessName endswith "mimikatz.exe"
  or CommandLine contains "Invoke-Mimikatz"
  or CommandLine matches regex @"(?i)comsvcs\.dll.*MiniDump.*lsass"
| extend AccountCustomEntity = Account
| extend HostCustomEntity = Computer
| project
    TimeGenerated,
    Computer,
    Account,
    NewProcessName,
    CommandLine,
    ParentProcessName
event.code: 4656 AND winlog.event_data.ObjectName: *lsass.exe* AND winlog.event_data.AccessMask: ("0x1010" OR "0x1410" OR "0x1438") AND NOT process.name: (svchost.exe OR csrss.exe OR wininit.exe)

event.category: "file" AND file.path: (*Chrome*"Login Data" OR *Firefox*"logins.json" OR *Edge*"Login Data") AND event.action: ("creation" OR "modification" OR "open") AND NOT process.name: (chrome.exe OR firefox.exe OR msedge.exe)

index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4656
| where ObjectName LIKE "%lsass.exe%"
| where AccessMask IN ("0x1010", "0x1410", "0x1438")
| where NOT ProcessName IN ("svchost.exe", "csrss.exe", "wininit.exe")
| stats count as AccessAttempts, earliest(_time) as FirstSeen, latest(_time) as LastSeen
  by host, Account, ProcessName, ObjectName
| where AccessAttempts > 3
| sort - AccessAttempts

index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4688
| where (CommandLine LIKE "%procdump%" AND CommandLine LIKE "%lsass%")
   OR (CommandLine LIKE "%comsvcs.dll%" AND CommandLine LIKE "%MiniDump%")
   OR NewProcessName LIKE "%mimikatz%"
| table _time, host, Account, NewProcessName, CommandLine, ParentProcessName
| sort - _time

fields @timestamp, @message
| filter @message like /lsass.exe/
  and (@message like /PROCESS_VM_READ/ or @message like /SeDebugPrivilege/)
| stats count() as AccessCount by host, user, process
| filter AccessCount > 3
| sort AccessCount desc

// Windows Event Forwarding / Sysmon Query
Event.EventData.EventID: 10 AND Event.EventData.TargetImage: "C:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe" AND Event.EventData.GrantedAccess: ("0x1010" OR "0x1410" OR "0x1438") AND NOT Event.EventData.SourceImage: ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe" OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\csrss.exe")
